summary:
The Tibetan Empire (618–842 AD) was the first unified Tibetan state — a powerful military kingdom founded by Songtsen Gampo (r. c. 617–649 AD) that at its peak controlled an enormous territory spanning the Tibetan plateau, parts of Central Asia, Nepal, and northwestern China, making it one of the major powers of the 7th–9th centuries alongside the Tang Dynasty and the Islamic Caliphate. The [[event:3522|Tang-Tibet Relations]] were a complex blend of warfare, diplomacy, and cultural exchange — the Tang and Tibet fought repeatedly over Central Asian trade routes, and Tibet even briefly captured the Tang capital Chang'an in 763 AD during the [[event:2153|An Lushan Rebellion's]] chaos.

Songtsen Gampo introduced Buddhism to Tibet (importing texts and monks from India and Nepal), commissioned the development of the Tibetan script (an alphabet created by the scholar Thonmi Sambhota to write Tibetan), and began the translation of Buddhist texts that would eventually make Tibet the great repository of Indian Buddhist learning. The great Tibetan Buddhist tradition — the 'third turning of the Dharma wheel' — was consolidated during the empire's reign, with the construction of Samye Monastery (779 AD, Tibet's first monastery) and the great translation projects that brought Indian Buddhist scholarship to the Tibetan plateau. The empire collapsed after the assassination of the last emperor Langdarma (842 AD), fragmenting into smaller principalities; Tibet would not be reunified politically until the 17th century under the Dalai Lama institution.
